observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct CENRO to closely coordinate with CPDO to increase awareness among implementing departments in the CCET process to ensure proper integration of CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs in the City’s plans;
Instruct the LCCAP Secretariat to closely coordinate with the implementing offices to prepare the M&E Report that includes all the CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs with complete details such as the allocated budget, actual cost incurred and status of PPAs to enable easier monitoring of utilization of fund and implementation of PPAs; and
Ensure that the planned CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs as indicated in the City’s LCCAP as integrated/mainstreamed in the AIP are implemented in accordance with RA No. 9729, as amended by 
RA No. 10174.
observations.managementAction:
On April 8, 2024, the City formally approved and adopted through Sangguniang Panlungsod (SP) Resolution No. 58-11, s. 2024, the 2023 to 2031 Pasig City LCCAP which coincides with the updated Comprehensive Land and Water Use Plan (CLWUP) as well as its Zoning Ordinance. Consequently, the City’s LCCAP served as their roadmap for action in the reduction of emission and outlined specific strategies and actions to formulate effective policies, programs and activities towards CC mitigation and adaptation.

Through EO No. PCG-18, s. 2024, dated April 25, 2024, the City reconstituted the Pasig City Climate Change and Greenhouse Gas Management Council which aims to strengthen the implementation of the CYs 2023 to 2031 LCCAP and streamline its future planning process.

On October 5, 2023, through SP Resolution No. 216-11, s. 2023, the City’s amended CY 2024 AIP was adopted and approved. Per review, the City was able to integrate CC responsive PPAs into its development plans.

Review/evaluation made of the City’s LCCAP disclosed that a total of 215 identified CC mitigation and adaptation PPAs with implementation timeframe of short-term (CYs 2023 to 2025), medium-term (CYs 2025 to 2027) and long-term (CYs 2023-2031). Thus, in its CY 2024 AIP, the City has identified 53 CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs with total approved appropriations amounting to P661,664,000.00.

The noted deficiencies in the planning, execution and monitoring of CC responsive PPAs hinder the determination of the actual utilization of the funds appropriated for LCCAP.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct CENRO to closely coordinate with CPDO to increase awareness among implementing departments in the CCET process to ensure proper integration of CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs in the City’s plans;
Instruct the LCCAP Secretariat to closely coordinate with the implementing offices to prepare the M&E Report that includes all the CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs with complete details such as the allocated budget, actual cost incurred and status of PPAs to enable easier monitoring of utilization of fund and implementation of PPAs; and
Ensure that the planned CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs as indicated in the City’s LCCAP as integrated/mainstreamed in the AIP are implemented in accordance with RA No. 9729, as amended by RA No. 10174.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the GFPS, TWG, CAO and City Budget Office to come up with standardize RC Code for GAD-related expenses that will be integrated in the computerized accounting system of the City GFPS to facilitate the generation of all reports pertaining to GAD-related expenses and other GAD-related financial transactions, and to monitor and properly account the utilization of the GAD funds
observations.managementAction: The CAO commented that it cannot extract all the GAD-related expenses as there no existing RC nor RC Codes created. However, capturing GAD-related expenses is included in the Terms of Reference in the updating of the City’s existing Computerized Information System which is currently on-going. The City’s offices also agreed that a standardized RC and RC Codes for GAD-related expenses should be implemented by all concerned offices to properly monitor and account the utilization of GAD funds.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that Management instruct:

The CAO to –
Conduct further verification and analysis of long outstanding Due to BIR account balances to finally settle the same and to avoid incurrence of unnecessary surcharges and interests that might be imposed by the BIR; and
Immediately remit the amount of P2,661,415.90 to BIR or make proper adjustments, if warranted.

The CTO to closely coordinate with and immediately inform the LRMO as regards release of checks pertaining to payment of DST, CGT and Estate Taxes for real properties bought or expropriated by the City; and
The LRMO to facilitate the immediate remittance to BIR of withheld taxes pertaining to real properties bought or expropriated by the City, particularly, DST and CGT which has been outstanding for four to six years.
observations.managementAction:
The CAO commented that:
The unremitted amount of P2,661,415.90 stated in the report was almost remitted in the month of March 2025 for the account of the GF;
The remaining amount to settle is for the SEF which is still ongoing reconciliation; and
This was the result of timing differences due to the accounting book was not yet closed during the remittance schedule.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct:

The CAO to –
To reconcile its records with GSIS and HRDO to facilitate full remittance of the employee’s and employer’s share premium contributions and loan amortizations of City personnel within the first 10 days of the calendar month following the month to which the contributions apply or when the deductions were effected; and
Determine and exert extra effort in tracing the details of the balances as well as the noted discrepancies pertaining to prior years’ unremitted accounts by reconciling the accounting and HRDO records with the statement of account from the GSIS.

The HRDO –
To ensure the downloading of the Monthly Electronic Billing File (EBF) from EBCS for reconciliation with the CAO to facilitate full remittance of the employee’s and employer’s share for premium contributions and loan amortizations of City personnel;
To take advantage of the feature of EBCS relating to uploading of agency’s ERF for validation against GSIS database to prevent rejection of remittances; and
To continue close coordination with GSIS until their personnel records are reconciled with that of the City to avoid or minimize unremitted balances.

The CAO and HRDO to meet with GSIS to discuss matters with respect to the GSIS account of the agency, particularly, premium arrearages for prior years.
observations.reason: Management still awaiting the national agencies’ confirmation

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct:

The HRDO to –
Request the latest Statement of Account (SOA) from Pag-IBIG Fund to determine the unremitted contributions and loan amortizations withheld by the City for current and prior years; and
Upon receipt of SOA, closely coordinate with the CAO for reconciliation of the same with accounting records to determine the actual amount that should be remitted to Pag-IBIG Fund.

The CAO to –
Validate and immediately remit to Pag-IBIG Fund the amount of P2,791,838.38 or make the necessary adjustments in the books, if warranted; and
Ensure that management remit the employer share and employees contributions to the Pag-IBIG Fund on time as prescribed by rules and regulations, together with payments made on loan amortizations, if any; and regularly reconcile its records with HRDO to avoid/prevent or minimize accumulation of unremitted payments to the Fund.
observations.managementAction:
The HRDO commented that:
Thru its Payroll Division, will coordinate with the liaison officer responsible for the Pag-Ibig Fund to request the SOA for the CYs 2023 to 2024;
Once the SOA was received, HRDO will immediately collaborate with the CAO to determine the actual amount that needs to be paid to the Pag-IBIG Fund; and
The HRDO is closely coordinating with the CAO as regards the unremitted amount to Pag-IBIG Fund.

The CAO commented that:
The CAO made an analysis on all transactions affecting Pag-Ibig contributions/loans per each SLs of previous year. They have verified one  transaction was made under JEV No. 100-2502-00559 dated 
February 28, 2025 in the amount of P2,012,240.92. With this adjustment, the unremitted balance of the Pag-Ibig contributions and loan for reconciliation with HRDO and Pag-IBIG is P798,044.78.
The CAO make it a point that employees/employer’s contributions deducted from their monthly payroll are to be remitted on a prescribed time. If there are contributions unremitted in a given month, this is due to timing differences.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct:

The HRDO to –
Request from PhilHealth SPA or the latest SOA to determine the unremitted premium contributions to the Corporation for current and prior years; and
Upon receipt of SPA/SOA, closely coordinate with the CAO for reconciliation of the same with accounting records to determine the actual amount that should be remitted to PhilHealth.

The CAO to –
Validate the SPA/SOA and immediately remit to PhilHealth the amount of P2,005,502.23 or make the necessary adjustments in the books, if warranted; and
Regularly reconcile its records with HRDO to avoid/prevent or minimize accumulation of unremitted premium payments to PhilHealth.
observations.managementAction:
The HRDO commented that:
In coordination with the CAO, the HRDO had verified the deficiency amounting to P2,005,502.63; and
Continuous and close coordination will be carried out with CAO and PhilHealth to determine the significant details on the remaining unremitted amount.

The CAO commented that:
The CAO responded that they are regularly communicating with the HRDO personnel for reconciliation of all mandatory contributions of employees; and
The unremitted PhilHealth contributions are for those newly hired employees, last salary, and differentials only. However, contributions deducted from the payroll, including employers’ share for existing employees (regular and casual/contractual), are remitted on a monthly basis.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the –

MISO to:
Establish the reliability of the reports generated by the RPAS by providing accurate data reflected in the Assessment Rolls and Summary of Receivables as to the total number of RPUs in the City; and
Coordinate with the end users of the RPAS to determine the reasons of contradicting data between the report generated by the CTO and the CAsO, and reconcile the same.
CAsO to:
Submit the Assessment Roll, in soft or hard copy, on or before the 31st day of December each year which will served as the basis by the CTO in preparing the duly certified list of taxpayer; and
Reconcile the RPUs reflected in the summarized Assessment Roll and the Summary of Receivables.
CTO to:
Prepare the duly certified list of taxpayer and Summary of Receivables based on the Assessment Roll submitted by the CAsO, copy furnished the CAO; and
In coordination with CAsO and MISO, provide details of current year’s receivables. Determine whether the “prior years’ delinquencies” RPUs were included or excluded in the “current year’s receivables” to establish the correct receivables at the beginning of the year.
observations.managementAction:
The MISO commented that they will upgrade the existing system and work closely with the system provider to ensure alignment with reporting requirements.

The CAsO commented that they have submitted the electronic copy of Assessment Roll.

The CTO commented that:
They have submitted to the CAO the duly certified list of taxpayers in soft copy and summary of receivables based on the Assessment Roll submitted by the CAsO; and
The RPT/SET Receivables set up at the beginning of the year represent only the taxes due for the current year, the “prior years’ delinquencies” RPUs are considered delinquent and accounted for separately.
observations.reason: The discrepancy of data as to the number of taxable RPUs between the RPT Division and CAsO should be reconciled.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct:
The CAO to –
Require the persons in charge of recording the PPE in the SLs, PPELC, and LS to coordinate with each other and reconcile their records;
Provide complete and adequate details of PPE in the LS;
Timely recognize PPE in the books; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries.
The OGS to –
Completely include PPE items with Property Cards to the RPCPPE; and
Regularly submit the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) together with its supporting documents to CAO for the timely recognition of PPEs.
CAO and OGS to –
Coordinate with one another to eliminate the discrepancies identified between the accounting and property records; and
Strictly follow the procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances.

We recommended that Management instruct:
OGS to –
Reconcile the RPCPPE with its internal or land database;
Ensure the proper reporting of the identified land in the RPCPPE;
Obtain/secure TCTs for all City-owned parcels of land to establish absolute ownership and mitigate possible legal risks;
Properly monitor the inventory of land titles and ensure safekeeping of the same; and
Submit and reconcile RPCPPE to CAO for proper recording thereof.
Appraisal Committee determine cost or fair valuation of the 255 parcels of land without acquisition cost/market value for proper recording/recognition in the books.
Implementing Office to –
Provide the details of the lands to be titled pursuant to Ordinance No. 42, s. 2024; and
Submit an update on the status of titling as of year-end including utilization of allocated funds.

We recommended that Management direct the OGS to make sure that all the insurable properties of the City are adequately covered or insured with the GIF of the GSIS in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that Management direct:
The CEO to –
Submit promptly the LOCIP as well as the COC and other supporting documents to the Auditor and to CAO for review and proper monitoring of the progress of the City’s infrastructure projects; and
Strictly monitor the status and progress of infrastructure projects to minimize/avoid further delay in completion thereof.
The CAO to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the total costs of completed projects from CIP accounts to the appropriate asset accounts based on the COC submitted by the CEO; and
CEO and CAO to properly reconcile the accounting records with the LOCIP.

We recommended that the Management instruct:
The OGS to submit to the CAO the SSMI together with the RIS bearing complete details according to the instruction provided in Annexes 35 and 33 of the MNGAS for LGUs, respectively, for the proper and timely recording of issuances of inventories; and
The CAO to review the SLCs for inventory accounts with dormant/ non-moving items, coordinate with the OGS whether these items are already consumed/issued, and effect the appropriate adjustments, if necessary.

We recommended that the Management instruct:
The OGS to –
Closely coordinate with the warehouse keepers regarding the acceptance of donated inventories from DOH and other donors, if any;
Ensure that all receipt and issuances of the donated inventories from all the donors are properly documented;
Regularly submit the PTR, AIR and SSMI together with their supporting documents to the CAO for the timely recording of the receipt and issuances of the inventories in the books; and
Include the unissued donated inventories on the RPCI, if any.
The CAO to update the books to reflect the receipt and issuance of donated items, upon receipt of the PTR, AIR and SSMI submitted by the OGS; and
The CHD to regularly and timely notify OGS of the receipt of the donated inventories.

We recommended that Management instruct the CAO to make the necessary adjusting journal entries for book reconciling items after preparing the BRS to reflect the accurate balances of Cash in Bank – Local Currency, including Current, Savings, and TD accounts.

We recommended that Management instruct the concerned AOs and CAO to:
Strictly enforce submission of the complete liquidation of CAs granted within the prescribed period;
Timely record the AOs’ liquidation in the books; and
Enforce immediate withholding of salaries of such AOs who failed to liquidate their CAs.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The CAO –
Promptly encode RPT collections in the accounting system to expedite the processing of the release of Barangay shares therefrom; and
Continuously coordinate with MISO regarding the updating/upgrading of the accounting system to prevent the manual determination and encoding of the Barangay where the property subject of the RPT is located and for convenient extraction of reports; and
The MISO to immediately refer the request of the CAO to the system provider to help facilitate the timely release of Barangay shares from RPT.

We recommended that Management instruct the CAO to:
Conduct a detailed analysis of the AP account and reclassify any amounts that were incorrectly classified to Other Payables or to their appropriate liability accounts, to ensure the accurate presentation of liabilities in the financial statements; and
Determine whether the dormant payables amounting to P62,538,500.92 represent valid and enforceable obligations of the City, thereafter, initiate the reversion of the said amount to the unappropriated surplus of the GF.

We recommended that Management direct the –
MISO to:
Establish the reliability of the reports generated by the RPAS by providing accurate data reflected in the Assessment Rolls and Summary of Receivables as to the total number of RPUs in the City; and
Coordinate with the end users of the RPAS to determine the reasons of contradicting data between the report generated by the CTO and the CAsO, and reconcile the same.
CAsO to:
Submit the Assessment Roll, in soft or hard copy, on or before the 31st day of December each year which will served as the basis by the CTO in preparing the duly certified list of taxpayer; and
Reconcile the RPUs reflected in the summarized Assessment Roll and the Summary of Receivables.
CTO to:
Prepare the duly certified list of taxpayer and Summary of Receivables based on the Assessment Roll submitted by the CAsO, copy furnished the CAO; and
In coordination with CAsO and MISO, provide details of current year’s receivables. Determine whether the “prior years’ delinquencies” RPUs were included or excluded in the “current year’s receivables” to establish the correct receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended that the Management direct the CLSB and PCED to –
Improve SEF utilization performance by maximizing the use of available funds to implement the priority PPAs indicated in the AIP; and
Ensure that PPAs in connection with the repairs and maintenance of school buildings are implementation-ready, thus, having a readily available POWs.

We recommended that the Management direct the CLSB to –
Ensure the timely preparation and approval of the SEF budget of the ensuing year on or before October 31 of the current year; and
Re-evaluate the priority programs funded by the SEF to ensure full compliance with the legal mandate governing the fund and to minimize the need for supplemental budget or augmentation for priority PPAs not initially included in the approved SEF Budget.

We recommended that Management direct:
The CEO to submit the notification of posting of signboards to COA within the prescribed period as required by Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013;
The CEO to provide the COA at the beginning of the year, with a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the current year; and
The concerned personnel/project engineers to check/monitor the contractor’s proper posting of signboards and to timely update the status of completion of the project as required by Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to direct/instruct the CEO and other implementing offices to expedite the completion of all ongoing projects funded under the 20% DF and enforce the imposition of liquidated damages on contractors responsible for significant delays.
Also, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management      instruct –
The City Planning & Development Office (CPDO) to strictly monitor the implementation of PPAs funded out of 20% DF to ensure alignment with the approved development plan; and
The PMO and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and implementing offices to ensure that the procurement process is conducted in accordance with the scheduled timelines of the approved PPAs.

In view hereof, we recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CDRRMO to execute a catch-up plan for the partially and unimplemented PPAs under LDRRMF and to properly monitor the efficient and full implementation of the priority PPAs in accordance with RA 10121; and
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the BAC in coordination with the end-user to expedite the procurement process to ensure the timely implementation of the planned PPAs and to avoid under-utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended the Management agreed to direct:
The CSWDO to promptly submit the purchase request and supporting documents to the PMO for the BAC to conduct bidding, and thus, to facilitate the timely processing and implementation of planned activities; and
The PMO to streamline and optimize purchase request and supporting documents’ review and validation process to ensure timely acquisition of supplies in accordance with the activity timelines outlined in the AIP.
We recommended and Management agreed to review and reconstitute the composition of the LCPC in compliance with DILG MC No. 2021-039 dated April 07, 2021.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct:
The SWMO to –
Coordinate with and provide the necessary assistance to the Barangay officials for the establishment of MRFs in every barangay or cluster of barangays; and
Regularly monitor the operation of the MRFs managed by the City and Barangays to ensure that the same is in good condition and used for the purpose for which the same is created.
The ABC President to remind Barangay officials to find/provide suitable space for MRF and ensure that their programs and projects relating to solid waste management are aligned with that of the City.

Instruct the LCCAP Secretariat to closely coordinate with the implementing offices to prepare the M&E Report that includes all the CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs with complete details such as the allocated budget, actual cost incurred and status of PPAs to enable easier monitoring of utilization of fund and implementation of PPAs; and

RA No. 10174.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GFPS and the GAD TWG to ensure that GAD PPAs are effectively and efficiently implemented within its target implementation period and make sure that the GAD budget is judiciously utilized as intended.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to direct/instruct the CEO and other implementing offices to expedite the completion of all ongoing projects funded under the 20% DF and enforce the imposition of liquidated damages on contractors responsible for significant delays.

Also, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management      instruct –

The City Planning & Development Office (CPDO) to strictly monitor the implementation of PPAs funded out of 20% DF to ensure alignment with the approved development plan; and

The PMO and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and implementing offices to ensure that the procurement process is conducted in accordance with the scheduled timelines of the approved PPAs.
observations.managementAction:
The City has appropriated funds in excess of the 20% minimum requirement set by law. Furthermore, the projects funded under the 20% DF were found compliant with rules and regulations, considering that projects identified therein are not among the prohibited or non-allowable expenditures listed in Item 3.2.5 of 
DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020.

Review and analysis of the City’s AIP and the submitted LOCIP vis-à-vis ocular inspection conducted in February 2025, revealed that 10 identified projects were initiated but not fully implemented in CY 2024, while six projects had not yet commenced.

The partial and non-implementation of these PPAs was attributed to encountered delays in the procurement process due to late submission of the request by the end-user to the Procurement Management Office (PMO) for the timely conduct of bidding plus glitches/problems faced during the bidding and site-related issues that necessitated design modifications to adapt to actual field conditions.

These issues had been consistently noted in the audit reports for CY 2023 and in previous years, yet it continues to persist. It is emphasized that timely implementation of planned PPAs is a critical element in government planning, as it ensures the delivery of intended benefits and contributes to achieving the City’s socio-economic and environmental development goals. The recurring delays, however, indicate the City's continued finding difficulty in executing its PPAs within the prescribed timelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CDRRMO to execute a catch-up plan for the partially and unimplemented PPAs under LDRRMF and to properly monitor the efficient and full implementation of the priority PPAs in accordance with RA 10121; and

We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the BAC in coordination with the end-user to expedite the procurement process to ensure the timely implementation of the planned PPAs and to avoid under-utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction:
For CY 2024, the City appropriated for LDRRMF a total of P654,331,386.00, equivalent to five percent of its estimated revenues from regular sources to support disaster mitigation, preparation, response and recovery. Of the total amount, P196,299,415.80 was allocated to 30% Quick Response Fund (QRF), while the remaining portion of P458,031,970.20 is allocated to 70% Mitigation Fund (MF), which is further broken down as follows: P247,585,045.20 for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) and P210,446,925.00 for Capital Outlay (CO).

Comparison of the submitted Local Disaster Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) and the Report on Utilization of DRMMF (RUD), both for CY 2024, is shown below. Analysis and verification of the LDRRMFIP and RUD, revealed an underutilization of P358,827,786.92 or 54.84 percent of its appropriation.

QRF Utilization: For the year 2024, the City has increased its utilization rate to 45.16 percent as compared to last year’s 13.05 percent. This is due to the significant increase in utilization of the QRF.

Underutilization of the 2024 LDRRMF: However, further analysis showed that a significant part of the underutilization came from the unexpended balance of MF. Expenditures charged against the MF included local and foreign traveling expenses, training expenses, procurement of equipment for the automation of gate controls, monitoring sensors and control center for the City’s pumping stations.

Unimplemented PPAs: Out of the 63 PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP, 21 PPAs with allocated budget amounting to P113,330,375.00 remained unimplemented during the year. These PPAs were not yet implemented due to difficulty in gathering quotation from various suppliers and/or inability to find a supplier that could provide all the needed supplies and materials for most PPAs.

In view hereof, we recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CDRRMO to execute a catch-up plan for the partially and unimplemented PPAs under LDRRMF and to properly monitor the efficient and full implementation of the priority PPAs in accordance with RA 10121; and
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the BAC in coordination with the end-user to expedite the procurement process to ensure the timely implementation of the planned PPAs and to avoid under-utilization of the LDRRMF.

observations.recommendation:

The CSWDO to promptly submit the purchase request and supporting documents to the PMO for the BAC to conduct bidding, and thus, to facilitate the timely processing and implementation of planned activities; and

The PMO to streamline and optimize purchase request and supporting documents’ review and validation process to ensure timely acquisition of supplies in accordance with the activity timelines outlined in the AIP.
observations.managementAction:
Verification of the Accomplishment Report of LCPC showed that P155,376,017.46 was utilized for CY 2024. This amounted to 7.05 percent of the total NTA, but when compared to total appropriations, it represents only 
56.76 percent. As a result, there remains an unutilized amount of P118,34,497.54 which is 43.24 percent, equivalent to the partially and not implemented LCPC PPAs.

Further review of the AWFP and the Accomplishment Report of LCPC showed that 20 PPAs with total budget of P45,971,460.00 were not implemented as of year-end.

Inquiry and verification revealed that the reasons for underutilization of the appropriated funds and non-implementation of planned PPAs were as follows:
Multiple exchanges of communication to revise the specifications of items in the purchase requests and terms of references; Failed biddings; Suspension of work because of an on-going project of DPWH in the area; Absence of involuntary commitment of children under protective custody; and Withdrawal of the supplier due to inability to perform its obligation.

We recommended the Management agreed to direct:
The CSWDO to promptly submit the purchase request and supporting documents to the PMO for the BAC to conduct bidding, and thus, to facilitate the timely processing and implementation of planned activities; and
The PMO to streamline and optimize purchase request and supporting documents’ review and validation process to ensure timely acquisition of supplies in accordance with the activity timelines outlined in the AIP.

The SWMO to –

The ABC President to remind Barangay officials to find/provide suitable space for MRF and ensure that their programs and projects relating to solid waste management are aligned with that of the City.
observations.managementAction:
One of said programs is the relaunching of the Basura palit Grocery Program [Mobile Recyclable Redemption Center], a project of the City in partnership with private sector, where residents are encouraged to bring recyclable/
non-perishable wastes in exchange for grocery items. This is part of the City’s campaign to reduce waste by encouraging Pasigueños to learn how to recycle and segregate at source while helping them in their grocery needs.

Furthermore, it is worthy to note that the City started shifting from conventional methods of waste reduction to a more sustainable model known as the circular economy.

The circular economy is a model of production and consumption, which involves sharing, leasing, reusing, repairing, refurbishing and recycling existing materials and products as long as possible. In this way, the lifecycle of products is extended.

Recently, the City established the Innovation for Circular Economy (ICE) Hub, the first-ever Circular Economy Hub [operated by the government] in the country. The ICE Hub is established in partnership with United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the Government of Japan and European Union to help in achieving one of the sustainable development goals of the United Nation (UN), that is, to ensure responsible consumption and production.

The above initiative of the City was even acknowledged by UN Secretary 
Deputy-General and Chair of UN Sustainable Development Group, 
Ms. Amina J. Mohammed, when she visited the ICE Hub in October 2024. Ms. Mohammed emphasized that the ICE Hub not only serves as a model for generating economic opportunities for women and other underserved communities, it is also one of the best illustrations for sustainable consumption and production.

These notable programs, among others, clearly show the City’s constant commitment to attain environmental sustainability for the growing population of the City.

For the City, the Chief of Solid Waste Management Office (SWMO) averred in a certification dated December 18, 2024 that the 30 Barangays of the City are covered with the services of MRFs, as follows: (list of MRFs)

On the other hand, this Office have conducted an independent evaluation and inspection from January 28 to February 19, 2025 to determine the existence and condition of these MRFs in thirty 30 Barangays.

It has been discovered that 14 out of 30 Barangays had no existing MRF. Moreover, the MRF of two other Barangays were not in good condition.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct:
The SWMO to –
Coordinate with and provide the necessary assistance to the Barangay officials for the establishment of MRFs in every barangay or cluster of barangays; and
Regularly monitor the operation of the MRFs managed by the City and Barangays to ensure that the same is in good condition and used for the purpose for which the same is created.
The ABC President to remind Barangay officials to find/provide suitable space for MRF and ensure that their programs and projects relating to solid waste management are aligned with that of the City.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CDRRMO to execute a catch-up plan for the partially and unimplemented PPAs under LDRRMF and to properly monitor the efficient and full implementation of the priority PPAs in accordance with RA 10121; and
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the BAC in coordination with the end-user to expedite the procurement process to ensure the timely implementation of the planned PPAs and to avoid under-utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction:
For CY 2024, the City appropriated for LDRRMF a total of P654,331,386.00, equivalent to five percent of its estimated revenues from regular sources to support disaster mitigation, preparation, response and recovery. Of the total amount, P196,299,415.80 was allocated to 30% Quick Response Fund (QRF), while the remaining portion of P458,031,970.20 is allocated to 70% Mitigation Fund (MF), which is further broken down as follows: P247,585,045.20 for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) and P210,446,925.00 for Capital Outlay (CO).

Comparison of the submitted Local Disaster Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) and the Report on Utilization of DRMMF (RUD), both for CY 2024, is shown below. Analysis and verification of the LDRRMFIP and RUD, revealed an underutilization of P358,827,786.92 or 54.84 percent of its appropriation.

QRF Utilization: For the year 2024, the City has increased its utilization rate to 45.16 percent as compared to last year’s 13.05 percent. This is due to the significant increase in utilization of the QRF.

Unimplemented PPAs: Out of the 63 PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP, 21 PPAs with allocated budget amounting to P113,330,375.00 remained unimplemented during the year. These PPAs were not yet implemented due to difficulty in gathering quotation from various suppliers and/or inability to find a supplier that could provide all the needed supplies and materials for most PPAs.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CDRRMO to execute a catch-up plan for the partially and unimplemented PPAs under LDRRMF and to properly monitor the efficient and full implementation of the priority PPAs in accordance with RA 10121; and
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the BAC in coordination with the end-user to expedite the procurement process to ensure the timely implementation of the planned PPAs and to avoid under-utilization of the LDRRMF.

Instruct the Local School Board and the City Budget Office to prepare and properly plan the SEF annual budget in accordance with the prescribed format as required under the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1; and
Henceforth, prepare the Annual Procurement Plan containing the list of items to be procured to support the SEF budget.

We recommended that Management instruct the responsible Offices to submit the approved POs and NODs to COA within the period prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 95-006 to enable the Auditor to conduct timely review and immediately communicate to Management any defects and/or deficiencies that may be found.

We recommended that Management instruct the concerned offices to submit to COA:
All contracts with complete integral parts, irrespective of amount involved, within five working days after perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
The Procurement Monitoring Report duly approved and signed by the Head of the Procuring Entity.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management instruct the City Engineer to:
Submit the 4th quarter list of on-going infrastructure projects for 
CY 2021;
Henceforth, submit the monitoring report on a monthly basis; and
Inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted.

We recommended that Management direct:
The City Engineer and the Medical Director of the PCGH to monitor the strict implementation of the PPAs funded out of the 20% DF pertaining to the procurement of infrastructure projects and medical equipment, respectively;
The Planning and Development Officer to ensure that the targeted PPAs do not coincide with the programs of other agencies; and
The Chairperson-Bids and Awards Committee to exercise its function effectively to avoid delay in the procurement process.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to COA the authority from the City Mayor pertaining to the duties being performed by the BAC which should have been undertaken by the CAO and GSO; and
Require the CDRRMO to monitor the implementation of the targeted PPAs to achieve the maximum utilization of the LDRRMF in compliance with the IRR of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that Management incorporate the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the LDRRMFIP in compliance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management instruct the concerned offices to attach all the necessary documents supporting the contract upon submission to COA to facilitate the proper technical review of the garbage contract pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management instruct:
The GAD Monitoring Team to prepare quarterly/semestral monitoring reports on the status of the implementation of GAD programs stating therein the reason/s for partial and/or non-implementation to ensure the complete and timely utilization of GAD budget;
The Chairperson-GAD Focal Point System-Technical Working Group (GFPS-TWG) to submit to COA the GAD Accomplishment Report and the quarterly/semestral monitoring reports on the implementation of GAD programs for CY 2021 and moving forward; and
The OIC-City Accountant to comply with COA Circular No. 2021-008 on the maintenance of SLs for GAD-related expenses to facilitate the monitoring, accounting and preparation of the GAD-related reports.
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management instruct the GFPS to set up appropriate systems to ensure the completion of the GAD database pursuant to Section 36(c) of RA No. 9710.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-City Accountant to:
Create an efficient monitoring system for the accurate and timely remittance of withheld taxes to the BIR; and
Exert more effort in tracing back the details of the net under-remittance totaling P256,025.88 pertaining to November 2020 and prior years’ withholding taxes.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management instruct:
The OIC-City Accountant to -
Remit in full the amounts withheld from the salaries of the City personnel on the month following the month in which withholding was made;
Trace the details of the balances pertaining to November 2019 and prior years by requesting the latest statements of account from the concerned government agencies.
The Human Resource and Development Officer (HRDO) to –
Make representation with the GSIS officials to sort out the issue of non-updating of members’ records in its website; and
Submit a list of employees without identification numbers to the PhilHealth to facilitate processing of the same.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Treasurer inform the BFP of the over remittance and to thereafter expedite deductions from subsequent collections; and
The collectors turn-over the Fire Inspection Fees to the City Treasurer and remittance to the BFP be made through the preparation of disbursement voucher and issuance of check payable to the agency to facilitate monitoring and prevention of over-remittance.

We recommended that the City Accountant endeavor to facilitate the analysis and classification of the infrastructure assets that need to be restored in the books of the City in order to reflect a more reliable PPE account balance in its financial statements; and
We also recommended the computation of the corresponding depreciation commencing when the City started using these assets.
We recommended that Management adequately insure all insurable property, assets and interests with the General Insurance Fund-Government Service Insurance System pursuant to RA No. 656.

We recommended that:
The Inventory Committee conduct a complete physical count of supplies and materials and prepare the RPCIs for two semesters in the prescribed format; and ensure that the balances are reconciled with the accounting records;
The Central Supply Officer -
Properly maintain the SCs for all types of inventory, fill up all the required information and regularly reconcile them to the SLCs;
The City Accountant –
Maintain SLCs for each inventory item, indicating the complete information on the specific IPSAS accounts they were recorded and year-end balances and ensure that the total of their balances reconcile with the GL; and
Ensure that the issuance of supplies and materials for distribution are supported with duly acknowledged distribution list of any equivalent document to prove that the items were received by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management strictly monitor the balances of inventories and ensure that the procurement of supplies and materials is adequately planned such that only those necessary in the operation of the requisitioning unit are procured in appropriate quantities at the right time.

We recommended that:
The City Treasurer furnish the City Accountant with a certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
The City Accountant set-up the RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year based on the certified list.

We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC and the CEO to submit a report on the nature of the recorded bidder’s bond and the status of projects covered by the performance bond, respectively, and recommend action/s to be taken thereon;
Furnish the CAO with the complete documents to serve as basis in effecting adjustments in the books; and
Henceforth, require the BAC and CEO to coordinate every time similar transactions arise so that appropriate actions are done at the right time.

We reiterated our recommendation that the CAO exert more effort to determine the details of the dormant accounts and gather relevant documents to facilitate the filing of requests for write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005. In the meantime, consider providing an allowance for impairment in accordance with IPSAS 21.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Planning and Development Office and City Engineer’s Office to properly coordinate with concerned offices/agencies to avoid duplication of projects and to ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU as required under the DBM-DILG JMC No. 2017-1.

We reiterate our previous recommendation that Management submit the GPB and the corresponding GAD AR within the prescribed period under COA Circular No. 2014-001 so that the timely review and evaluation of the same may be undertaken as well as the determination of whether the GAD budget was used judiciously, effectively and efficiently to address gender issues.
We recommended that the Management formulate the IRR and thereafter publish and disseminate the same pursuant to the above-mentioned MC for effective implementation of the GAD Code.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City prioritize the establishment and maintenance of the GAD Database to serve as basis in the identification of priority gender issues that are crucial for the City’s evidence-based planning and policymaking pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM–NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that:
The Management present the GAD PPAs in the AIP in such manner that effective gender mainstreaming in the City’s development plan can easily be identified and assessed;
The PPAs included in the GAD Plan and Budget be implementable and properly funded to avoid unutilized funds or incurrence of overdraft. These should also be aligned with the AIP down to the Accomplishment Report in accordance with relevant provisions of the PCW - DILG - DBM – NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 in order to facilitate review and assessment.  The use of funds for PPAs that will not be implemented is likewise discouraged.
Lastly, should the City determine that they need additional budget for specific programs, comply with paragraph 2 item 4.1.C.7 of PCW – DILG – DBM – NEDA - JMC No. 2013-01 which states that the LGUs, through their LCEs, shall inform the DILG Regional Office in writing if there are changes in the DILG-approved GPB.
We recommended that the Management subject the attributed projects to HGDG tests as prescribed by the JMC above.

We recommended that the City ensure that the OCD and DILG-LGOO be furnished a copy of the AIP within the prescribed deadline in order that they may perform their oversight functions pursuant to NDRRMC – DBM– DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that Management, upon identification of the PPAs to be funded out of the 1% EDF, prepare the Agency Plan for Older Persons and Agency Plan for Persons with Disabilities in the required format and submit the same to the DSWD and NCWDP, respectively.  The Accomplishment Report should likewise be prepared and submitted on a semestral basis.  These documents will help facilitate the assessment/evaluation of the relevance of the PPAs, the status of implementation and whether the identified areas of concern are addressed.

We recommended that the PDAO prioritize the development of its database that should contain relevant information to account for all PWDs in the City and facilitate information dissemination to ensure that bona fide PWDs benefit from the PPAs intended for them.

We recommended that the City:
Fast track the development of its Comprehensive Local Juvenile Intervention Program (CLJIP) so that the Pasig City Council for the Protection of Children (PCCPC) could perform its mandated tasks; and
Ensure that the appropriated 1% LCPC fund will be exclusively used for PPAs involving juvenile intervention and delinquency prevention.

We recommended that the City Mayor order the reconstitution of and convene the City Solid Waste Management Board to address the challenges on solid waste management issues being faced by the LGU and for the Board to resume performance of its mandate towards the protection of the health and environment of its constituents in accordance with RA No. 9003.

We recommended that:
The CAO and the Human Resource Management Office ensure the withholding of correct taxes on compensation and remittance of the same within the prescribed period, and facilitate the collection of tax deficiencies and refund of excess taxes to the concerned employees; and
The CAO remit the full amount of taxes withheld during the month on or before the 10th day of the next month to avoid over or under remittance.  There should only be one remittance for taxes withheld on compensation and one for the expanded withholding tax to facilitate reconciliation of the amount withheld with the amount remitted, and prepare adjusting entries, when necessary to ensure accuracy of the account balance.

We recommended that Management require the HRMO and the CAO to:
Ensure that the correct contributions are deducted and remitted to the respective agencies within the prescribed period; and
Reconcile their records with the concerned agencies, and effect the necessary adjustments, if any.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Require the City General Services Officer to exert more effort to come up with a more reliable RPCPPE complete with specifications and land use based on actual physical count and reconcile the same with the accounting records including the acquisition cost or fair value whichever is applicable; and
Direct the City Accountant to-
Maintain and update the Real Property Ledger Cards;
Coordinate with CGSO in reconciling the records with the physical count; and
Effect the necessary adjustments in the books so that reliable balance of Land account is presented in the City’s financial statements.

We recommended that Management strictly monitor the balances of inventories and ensure that the procurement of supplies and materials is adequately planned such that only those necessary in the operation of the requisitioning unit are procured in appropriate quantities at the right time.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Classify payables in their appropriate accounts according to the nature of transactions;
Ensure that the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) on the set up and settlements of payables are supported with complete documentation and with information on the details of the related transaction; and
Properly maintain the AP Aging Schedule with complete information as to the age, status and other relevant information.

We recommended that the City Accountant record in the books all unreleased checks at the end of the year, to ensure that the transactions and events covered by these checks are recorded during the year when they were occurred and recognized in the financial periods to which they relate.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 656 and  Circular No. 2018-002 dated  for the guidelines in the insurance of government properties with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

We recommended that:
The City Council revisit the ordinances and review the accomplishments for possible adjustment in appropriation deemed excessive which can be reprogrammed for other programs/projects or formulate new policies that will maximize the fund utilization in favor of the target beneficiaries of the project;
The City Accountant locate the documents supporting the recorded receivables to facilitate collection, identify the causes of the noted discrepancies and effect adjustments where necessary to arrive at reliable account balances; and
The responsible officers exert more effort to collect the outstanding accounts and act fast on delinquencies to make the borrowers aware that the loans are collectible when due and to discourage others from not paying their loans.

We recommended that Management:
Institute action to collect the outstanding loans; and
Decide on whether to continue the implementation of the program or to revert the unexpended balance to the General Fund so it can be used for other purposes.

We recommended that the City General Services Office:
Keep an inventory/stock on hand of the necessary and  frequently used materials for immediate repair; and
Separate the procurement of materials needed for repair and maintenance chargeable against the General Fund and the Special Education Fund for shorter procurement period.

We recommended that the responsible offices implement strategies that will further improve collections, particularly on those revenues where collections decreased during the year and regularly monitor the trend so that alternative actions can be undertaken on a timely manner to lessen or avoid under collection.

We recommended that the City ensure the feasibility of the selected projects and observe timeliness in their implementation in order to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.

We recommended that the PCDRRMO:
Prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002, ensuring that the implementation and itemization of PPAs are aligned, and furnish the same to the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD) and the Department of the Interior and Local Government – Local Government Operations Office (DILG-LGOO); and
Coordinate with the Bids and Awards Committee to include the LDRRMFIP/AIP in the consolidation of the APP.
We recommended that the City Accountant timely prepare and submit the RUDRRMF to the City Auditor’s Office for review pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that the City Accountant rely on the RUDRRMF in determining the correct unexpended LDRRMF balance to be transferred to the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the City Accountant use the appropriate accounts provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002 in recording the transfer of unexpended LDRRMF balances to the Trust Fund so that reliable account balances are presented in the financial statements.
We recommended that the PCDRRMO continue its effort to implement the programs, projects and activities charged to the 5% LDRRMF.

We recommended that Management submit the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding GAD Accomplishment Report (GAD AR) to the concerned offices within the prescribed period so that the timely review and evaluation of the same aimed at determining the judicious use of the GAD budget and the efficiency and effectiveness of interventions in addressing gender issues can be undertaken.
We recommended that the Management formulate the IRR and publish and disseminate the same pursuant to the above-mentioned Memorandum Circular for effective implementation of the GAD Code.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City prioritize the establishment and maintenance of the GAD Database to serve as basis in the identification of priority gender issues that are crucial for the City’s evidence-based planning and policymaking.
We recommended that the Management prepare the PIMME/FIMME checklist to identify the extent of accomplishment reported and gender-responsiveness of the attributed programs to GAD Budget.

We recommended that the Management continue its efforts to implement GAD PPAs and properly prepare the GAD Accomplishment Report to reflect only the costs of PPAs actually incurred during the year, duly supported with complete documents.

We recommended that the City prepare and submit the Agency Plan for both the SC and the PWD, not only for submission to the DSWD and the NCWDP as required by the JC, but also as basis in facilitating the assessment/evaluation of the status of program/project implementation.

We recommended that the City, thru the Pasig City Council for the Protection of Children, endeavor to come up with specific guidelines, policies and procedures pertaining to the PPAs charged to the 1% LCPC Fund duly supported with comprehensive data to warrant that these intervention programs will satisfy the needs of the CAR and CICL.

We recommended that:
The Accounting Office, in coordination with the HRMO, effect the tax refund on or before January 25 of the following year; and make the necessary arrangement with employees with tax payable on the terms of payment for the amount of taxes paid by the City in their behalf; and
The HRMO to require the Payroll System Provider to allow the generation of payslips and have them disseminated to each employee at least every payroll month so they become aware of their monthly earnings and deductions.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office and HRMO coordinate closely in working on the monthly withholding tax of employees and tax remittances to the BIR, see to it that their respective personnel tasked to deduct and/or remit taxes exercise care and diligence so that correct taxes are withheld and remitted on time to avoid discrepancies and penalties for late remittances.  The Accounting personnel in charge of withholding taxes from suppliers/contractors, likewise, ensure that appropriate taxes are withheld and remitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the City General Services Officer (GSO) to ensure that each piece of property acquired by the City is covered with a TCT in its name; conduct a physical inventory of city-owned real properties and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE complete with all the required details particularly the area, location and actual use/condition ensuring that only items actually counted/inspected are listed; and coordinate with the City Accountant in reconciling the RPCPPE with the accounting records; and in case of discrepancies, assist in finding the cause/s and suggest/work out for solutions that will make both records reliable, complete and in agreement; and
Direct the City Accountant to see to it that transactions affecting Land are supported with complete documents and correctly recorded; to maintain Real Property Ledger Card; to coordinate with the GSO in reconciling the property records with the physical count; and to effect the necessary adjustments in the books so that the reliable balance of Land account is presented in the City’s financial statements.

We recommended that:
The Inventory Committee include the supplies and materials in the conduct of physical count as well as the preparation and submission of the inventory report;
The City General Services Officer and the City Accountant properly maintain an updated stock cards and supplies ledger cards, respectively, and reconcile these records regularly; and
The City General Services Officer, the Central Supply Officer and the City Accountant come up with a system that will ensure that all deliveries are recorded in the books as soon as they are inspected/accepted before they are issued to end-users.

We recommended that Management ensure that procurement of supplies and materials is adequately planned such that only those necessary in the operation of the requisitioning unit are procured in appropriate quantities at the right time taking into consideration the provisions of Republic Act (RA) 9184. It is further recommended that the City Accountant see to it that the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) issued to record issuance/reduction in inventory are supported with IAR, SSMIs, RIS and all the required documents that will prove the propriety of the entry.

We recommended that the City Accountant effect the necessary adjustments based on the identified errors and exercise diligence and adhere to the applicable provisions of law and accounting standards in recording transactions.

We recommended that the City Accountant record in the books all unreleased checks at the end of the year, to ensure that the transactions and events covered by these checks are recorded during the year when they occur and recognized in the financial periods to which they relate.
We recommended that the concerned City officials refrain from resorting to the reimbursement scheme in paying gasoline, oil and lubricants and strictly conform with the requirements of RA 9184.

The City Accountant immediately record the medical equipment in the books pursuant to PPSAS 17;
The GSO issue the ARE to establish the accountability of each personnel responsible for these items; and
The City Accountant and the GSO to coordinate and come up with a policy on how PPE items received by the city from whatever source can be accounted for and recognized in the books of accounts at the appropriate time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to direct the CAO to:
Enforce the immediate settlement/liquidation of all outstanding CAs of the concerned accountable officers and employees pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Impose sanctions by withholding salaries of accountable officers who fail to effect prompt liquidation pursuant to 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the CAO consistently exercise care, observe prudence in keeping accounting records and ensure timely accounting of transactions for fair presentation of the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:
The bookkeepers in-charge in booking up transactions exercise due diligence in the performance of their duties. Such diligence has to be coupled with a quality review by those in-charge to forestall the occurrence of errors; and
The CAO effect adjustments to present the correct balances of the accounts affected.

We recommended that Management direct:
The CGSO and the CAO to prioritize the reconciliation of PPE records they maintain; verify and analyze the discrepancies to arrive at the correct balances of PPE accounts presented in the financial statements; and, henceforth, keep up reconciled reports at all times.
The CAO to initiate the dropping off from the books of accounts of the disposed PPE items amounting to P88,108,514.71.
The CGSO to facilitate the submission of the necessary reports/documents for the obsolete and unserviceable properties worth P96,009,992.30 to serve as basis of the CAO in the derecognition of the items from the books of accounts as provided in paragraph 82 of PPSAS No. 17.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:
The CAO ensure timely accounting of transactions for accurate financial reporting for each period, which allows inter-period comparability in order for the management and other stakeholders to derive meaningful conclusions about the trends in the City’s financial statements over time;
The CAO set up an effective and efficient reporting system to address the noted weakness and preclude or at the very least, minimize omissions, for fair presentation of the City’s financial statements; and
All concerned offices/units make as part of their regular process on a monthly basis, the immediate submission of copies of the AIR, sales invoices/billings, payroll registers and other similar documents to the CAO for proper recording of assets/expenses and the outstanding liabilities of the City.

We recommended that Management include the ordinances enacted/amended from CYs 1992 to 2015 in coming up with the revised Revenue Code.  We also suggest that the existing articles and provisions be revisited with the end-in-view of considering the call of the present times towards the best interest of the public and the government.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that management revisit the provisions of the Pasig Revenue Code with the end in view of updating the structure of tax rates for each well-defined business, clothing its action with the express provision of the Local Government Code.

We recommended that the CT refrain from granting tax relief without the corresponding authorizing ordinance and ensure that all monies owed to the City are collected.  Further, if the CTO finds the necessity for abatement of penalties, recommendation to the SP of Pasig City should be made for the enactment of the enabling ordinance.  Furthermore, the CTO should install an effective mechanism to objectively evaluate the cause of the taxpayer’s delinquency that gave rise to the exaction of penalties before a recommendation is made for the condonation thereof.

We take positive note of the above accomplishments of the City and encourage management to continue its efforts to pursue the implementation of the approved programmed projects and activities for GAD, to ensure attainment of the objectives of the covering law for the ultimate benefit of the target constituents.

We recommended that the PCCPC performs its mandated functions set under DILG MC 2008-126 by preparing the AWFP for the 1 percent LPCP funds for incorporation to the City’s LDIP/AIP; and approving fully deliberated PPAs to be implemented for the year rightly contained in a resolution duly passed in order to lay down the specific details of the PPAs.
We recommended that management properly plan the PPAs of the LCPC and utilize the fund in order to fully deliver the services intended for the benefit of the children.

We recommended that Management:
Make the necessary steps for the proper implementation of Section 5 of RA No. 656; and
Ensure that all insurable properties of the City are insured.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City thru the Human Resource Department (HRD):
Ensure compliance to the Revised Omnibus Rules on Appointment and Other Personnel Actions, and the Guidelines on Designation of the CSC in all future designations to be issued; and
Consider reviewing/revisiting its existing structure and manpower vis-à-vis its realistic needs in order to maximize operational efficiency.

We recommended that:
The CGP workout for collaborative efforts with the DepEd officials and the PGR regarding the construction in the RHS;
The concerned officials address the deficiency and non-compliance noted in the contract provisions and to be more cautious in the preparation of contracts; and
The CAO record the projects of the City with particularity, e.g. per building and location, for clear identification and facility of monitoring the project.

We recommended that the CAO religiously remit the monthly GSIS contributions, premium payments, loan amortizations and other amounts withheld from employees in full and within the required period to ensure that employees enjoy the full benefits from the GSIS, avoid penalties and the possible non-approval of employee loans.  Otherwise, appropriate penal sanctions may be imposed upon the City officials for violating pertinent provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 8291.

In light of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Adequately plan, including the conduct of a tailor-fitted feasibility study for each project chargeable against the 20 percent CDF to anticipate identifiable problems and potential project delays, among others.  Consider the advice and assistance provided by the appropriate government offices on specialized projects to be undertaken by the City;
Timely implement projects under the CDF in the year this was appropriated to seasonably provide the pronounced benefits for the constituents; and
See to it that the 20 percent CDF is optimally utilized so as to achieve the intended purpose for which it was allocated.
We recommended that the PCDC abide by its functions as mandated in Section 109 (3) and (5) of R.A. 7160 by coordinating with the City Planning and Development Office in the preparation of detailed reports that support the determination and implementation of the priority project in order to properly justify and closely monitor the projects charged against the 20 percent CDF.
We recommended that any change, specifically expansion of scope of priority projects have to be presented for approval of the PCDC and the SP as required under RA 7160.

We recommended that the CHO be an active front liner in determining the needs of all the offices and units, including the BHCs, BSHCs and other special health clinics, under its jurisdiction, fully recognizing that each unit has its share in the overall health mission of the City.

We recommended that the CHO exercise utmost care and diligence in the preparation and review of the PPMPs, complete with required information under the pertinent provisions of the RIRR of RA 9184, to ensure that the overall targets of the CHO is achieved effectively.
We also recommended that in future preparation of the PPMP, the requirements of the health centers be considered based on a duly verified consumption report together with active consultation and participation of the concerned health workers to ensure fulfillment of the objectives of the Health Services of the City and provision of adequate and appropriate medicines.
We recommended that Management:
Judiciously plan the PPMP to ensure that procurements and planned requirements are met in accordance with documented results of needs assessment;
Consider the past data of issuances, utilization as well as the available balances/inventory levels of the items at the time of procurement and the requirement of end-users including the barangay health centers and inputs of the Department of Health in the planning for the procurement of drugs and medicines;
Exercise constant monitoring of procurement levels and balances of drugs and medicines so that only the needed drugs and medicines with the appropriate quantities are procured; and
Follow-up the replacement of the returned drugs and medicines from the suppliers concerned so as not to get them expired.

We recommended that the CHO require the different BHCs and other units/offices to submit monthly reports on the actual utilization of drugs and medicines for the complete and accurate recording of issuances by the Accounting Department.
We recommended the following:
The CHO to update the report on the receipt of drugs and medicines based on the Invoice Receipt of Property issued by the DOH;
The CAO to record in the books the receipts as well as issuances, of drugs and medicines and all other items from the DOH submitted by the CHO for complete reporting of inventories to conform with Section 63 of P.D. 1445 and Section 375 (d) of RA 7160; and
The CHO and the CAO workout to capture in the existing system the receipt of drugs and medicines and other medical supplies or equipment from all sources.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the CHO:
Review the circulars and guidelines issued by PhilHealth for the proper utilization of the amount transferred to the City;
Closely coordinate with the barangay health centers, the animal bite treatment center and the CHAMP center and determine their needs that can be funded by the appropriations of the City and the funds transferred by PhilHealth so that the government’s goal of providing medical services to the indigents and the City’s constituents will be fully implemented, and thus, best results could be achieved in the most opportune time; and
Prepare and submit the PFP-DAF in accordance with the PhilHealth guidelines.
We also recommended that CAO:
Record the amount received separately for operational costs (purchase of medicines and other administrative costs) and for honoraria per provider and according to purpose in order to fully account for the receipt and utilization of the amounts received from PhilHealth;
Reclassify the Other Payables account to the Prior Period Adjustment account, thus making the facility fee received in CY 2016 and prior years available for appropriation; and
Henceforth, properly account and appropriately record in the books the amount received for facility fee from PhilHealth as Hospital Fees.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the:
Revenue Generating Units:
Formulate fitting strategies, including effective and creative promotions, to consistently increase collections and achieve the targets set by the LFC; and
Properly lay down revenue target proposals, not only based on past year’s actual collections, but anchored on updated databases of revenue source specifically highlighting significant points and changes during the collection year for consideration of the Local Finance Committee.
Local Finance Committee exercise close coordination with the revenue generating units to come up with realistic and verifiable targets, giving due consideration to all points raised, in order to maximize the collection potential of the City;
City Treasurer’s Office:
Coordinate with the City Accounting Office for the reconciliation of the revenue items in the Revenue Collection Report and the Statement of Financial Performance to come up with an appropriate tool for informed decision-making.; and
Ensure the proper and consistent treatment of revenue items following the definitions provided under the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units to keep aligned with the accounting treatment of the items.
City Accounting Office:
Constantly collaborate with collecting offices for the proper treatment and reporting of income collections; and
Properly account and appropriately record in the books the amount received for payment of patients’ hospital dues from PhilHealth as Hospital Fees.
We further recommended that the CAO:
Properly record refunds made to PhilHealth members as deductions from outstanding other payables to PhilHealth; and
Verify the negative balance and make appropriate adjustment in the accounts.

The City Accounting Office to:

Analyze and adjust the identified reconciling items for fair presentation of the Cash in Bank account balance;

Make arrangement with the depository banks to provide copies of the bank debit and credit memoranda to the City on a monthly basis or on a more convenient frequency to properly document the recording of the items in the books of accounts; and

Prepare and submit for audit the Bank Reconciliation Statements on a regular basis pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

The City Treasury Office:

Keenly monitor outstanding checks and immediately inform the City Accounting Office of all staled checks for appropriate adjustment in the accounting record.
